text.title: Thin Views Pattern
text.summary: APIView pattern: validate input, call service, return response. Every endpoint requires @extend_schema with summary, request, responses, and tags.

rule.statement: Views handle exactly three concerns: (1) validate request input via serializer, (2) call a service function, (3) return the response. All business logic lives in services.
rule.rationale: Fat views (business logic in def post) are untestable from Celery or management commands. Thin views are unit-testable; services are independently testable and reusable.

rule.statement: Services must accept domain types (user_id: int or User model), never request.user directly. This allows calling the same service from Celery, management commands, and tests.
rule.rationale: request.user is an HTTP concern. Service functions called from Celery tasks have no request object. Passing request.user to services couples them to the HTTP layer.

example.code:
from rest_framework.views import APIView from rest_framework.response import Response from rest_framework import status from drf_spectacular.utils import extend_schema
from apps.users import services as user_services from . import serializers
class ItemActivationView(APIView): @extend_schema( summary="Activate item", description="Activates item for authenticated user", request=serializers.ItemActivationRequest, responses={200: serializers.ItemActivationResponse}, tags=["Items"], ) def post(self, request): # 1. Validate input serializer = serializers.ItemActivationRequest(data=request.data) serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)
# 2. Call service (pass domain type, not request.user) item = user_services.activate_user_item( user_id=request.user.id, item_code=serializer.validated_data["item_code"], )
# 3. Return response return Response( serializers.ItemActivationResponse(item).data, status=status.HTTP_200_OK, )
